transcript_events.text: The scrollbar for the Supervisor tab in the toolbar for the web surface is on the wrong element; it should be on the .chat-output-box. Create a refine goal for this.
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’ll create one narrowly scoped Refine goal for moving the Supervisor tab’s scrolling boundary to `.chat-output-box`, with browser verification in the acceptance criteria. I’ll first confirm the local goal-creation interface and current state so I don’t bypass supervision or duplicate an existing goal.

No existing goal matches this scrollbar ownership bug. I found related Supervisor UI goals, but they cover tab structure, input behavior, lifecycle controls, and status—not which element owns scrolling—so this is a distinct goal. I’m recording the implementation boundary and browser-level acceptance evidence without starting or promoting the goal.

Created Refine goal `00000SZ2PGMQR29F16GSKJ4000`: “Put the Supervisor chat scrollbar on `.chat-output-box`.”

It includes behavioral browser coverage and focused test requirements. Refine automatically claimed it as `in-progress` on branch `refine/00000SZ2PGMQR29F16GSKJ4000/round-2`; I did not manually start, merge, or approve it.

Created Refine goal `00000SZ2PGMQR29F16GSKJ4000`: “Put the Supervisor chat scrollbar on `.chat-output-box`.”

It includes behavioral browser coverage and focused test requirements. Refine automatically claimed it as `in-progress` on branch `refine/00000SZ2PGMQR29F16GSKJ4000/round-2`; I did not manually start, merge, or approve it.
Provider turn completed.
Sent 2 queued messages to the provider.

I’ll supervise the two live goals and the three queued retries through Refine’s own state and process surfaces. I’ll treat the newest snapshot as authoritative, preserve confirmation boundaries, and report any provider/authentication or recovery failure instead of masking it.

The latest evidence supersedes the earlier failed-count snapshot: the three retryable goals are queued as `todo`, while two workflow agents are live. I’m now checking the durable goal records, agent/process leases, and audit tail directly; I won’t infer health merely from the aggregate counts.

Refine has already replenished capacity: all three queued retries were claimed, so there are now five live workflow agents and zero queued work. The worker processes and their five goal-specific worktrees are present; no stall/loss evidence is visible yet. I’m continuing through review, merge, and cleanup rather than treating “claimed” as completion.

All five provider processes remain alive and are producing output; their logs show active repository/test commands rather than authentication or launch failures. The cross-process coordination goal is editing its isolated worktree; the other four are still in investigation/implementation. No confirmation request or destructive recovery action is warranted.

Two goals have cleared implementation, governance, and Quality and are now in `review`; three remain active. I’m auditing the review evidence and Refine’s normal transition commands before approving anything—`review` is not being treated as an automatic merge authorization.

The scrollbar goal was approved through Refine’s normal non-force path: its candidate merged cleanly, pushed to `origin/main`, and its worktree was cleaned only after merge. One separate goal—autonomous failed-Goal recovery—has now failed its validation and is explicitly visible as retryable; I’m inspecting that failure before deciding whether an evidence-backed retry is appropriate. The no-op Quality UX review remains unapproved because its own report says its prerequisites are not yet on `main`.

The failed recovery goal is intentionally parked behind the still-running shared-coordination goal; its retry would currently reproduce the same two evidence-integrity violations, so I am not cycling it. The two remaining workers are healthy and making progress in their isolated worktrees. One review is still held because it produced no implementation and explicitly requires the shared Quality evaluator first.

Both live goals remain within their configured 900-second stall window. Shared coordination is running the full library test set after format/diff checks; the Quality evaluator goal has recovered from an intermediate compile error and is continuing its implementation. No provider/authentication failure has occurred.

The two active agents surfaced ordinary test failures, not provider failures: coordination fixed a lowered-concurrency regression and is rerunning the full library suite; Quality is correcting its focused Quality tests. I’m leaving both under their managed processes and preserving their worktrees.

Shared coordination has completed implementation and committed candidate `bfdfc92`; Refine is now advancing it into independent governance/validation. The Quality goal is still resolving a focused retry-path regression. I will not retry the parked recovery goal until the coordination candidate is actually reviewed and merged.

Quality’s retry-path regression is now green after correcting runtime-root error handling; the agent is auditing removal of legacy command/settings paths before broader verification. Shared coordination remains in independent governance review.

Quality’s focused retry path now passes end to end with the failed candidate preserved; it has started the full library suite. Coordination governance is still actively checking recovery/process ownership paths and has not produced a verdict yet.

Independent governance rejected coordination candidate `bfdfc92` with three concrete issues: continuous owner-loss recovery, semantic conflict recheck after non-fast-forward state pushes, and missing concurrent failure-path tests. This is a legitimate in-scope retry—not a provider failure or a request for destructive authority—so I’m preserving the candidate, recording those findings as a new round, and requeueing it through Refine. The dependent recovery goal remains parked.

Refine accepted the new round but correctly refused `goal start` because the Goal is still `failed`; no worktree or merge was touched. I’m locating the existing failed-Goal recovery transition rather than bypassing the workflow state machine or editing durable JSON directly.

The new coordination round is now running in a fresh isolated round-7 worktree via Refine’s shared “last workflow state” recovery path; no durable JSON was edited directly. Current state is two active, zero queued, one parked failure, and one held review.

Quality’s full test run is green and it is doing a final quiet confirmation. Coordination round 7 is actively implementing the exact owner/provider and concurrent-push gaps from governance. State remains two active, zero queued, with the dependent recovery failure still deliberately parked.

Quality has also passed both normal and ignored full-workflow integration paths, plus final format/diff/legacy-path checks. It is at final review/commit; coordination remains active in its targeted fix.

Coordination has isolated the lost-owner defect to exact claim/lease evidence and is running the first targeted recovery test after patching. Quality is tightening test evidence after its green integration run; both remain under the managed workflow processes.

Both targeted gates are green so far: coordination’s live-provider and same-record conflict checks pass; Quality’s retry test and clippy pass, followed by a full-suite rerun after its final evidence changes. No merge or approval is being attempted before governance.

Coordination’s new genuinely concurrent same-record push test is green, and it is moving into cleanup/final verification. Quality’s integration tests remain green; the agent is doing a final shared-service ownership audit before committing.

The latest targeted fixes are green. Coordination’s one failed command was only an invalid combined test-filter invocation; the same tests passed when run separately. Quality added and passed a regression proving provider-reported success is rejected when supervised execution fails, then passed the full library suite.
Quality candidate `a40ab0e` is committed and has entered independent governance; coordination is still in implementation verification. Both remain unmerged and reviewable.

Coordination’s real two-daemon owner-loss, capacity-release, and synchronized push-race harnesses pass; format, diff, and all-target checks also pass. It is running the serial full library gate. Quality governance is auditing API/CLI/web ownership and retry semantics.

Coordination’s serial library and full-workflow gates are green; after a small final adjustment its focused recovery/conflict checks are green again. Quality governance’s targeted evidence-integrity and retry-path tests also pass.

A supplemental independent review caught an additional coordination crash window where `Goal=Failed` could persist before the claim became `Interrupted`; the active agent is fixing it before committing. Quality governance found no repository-evidence path under the searched ownership area (the search exited 1 for no matches) and is continuing its test audit.

The newly found crash window now has a passing regression that repairs a terminal Goal with a stranded running claim. Coordination is rerunning the complete serial gate; Quality governance is checking docs/intent provenance and cross-surface parity.

Quality governance rejected `a40ab0e` on two concrete issues: retry must preserve the failed round’s original Quality timing, and passing evidence must prove the worktree remained exactly at the candidate commit with no dirty mutation before/after execution. I’m preserving the candidate, recording those findings as a new round, and requeueing through the same shared recovery transition.

Coordination’s new ownerless-terminal test exposed an invalid test setup transition (`Todo→Cancelled/Failed`), not a provider or production failure; the agent is correcting the fixture. Quality round 3 cleanly merged its preserved candidate into the fresh isolated branch and is implementing only the two governance fixes.

Coordination’s corrected ownerless-terminal fixture passes and the full serial gate is still running. Quality has located existing Git cleanliness helpers and is wiring candidate/head/dirty checks into the shared Quality service rather than adding surface-specific logic.

Coordination’s 434-test serial gate is green and both production harnesses have completed; the agent is doing final scoped review before commit. Quality is updating the shared claim/context schema so retry timing is preserved durably.
Coordination candidate `ec0d336` is committed cleanly and has entered a fresh independent governance pass. Quality round 3 is still implementing its two scoped fixes.

Coordination governance is actively inspecting the new Git conflict and recovery state transitions. Quality has begun applying the timing and candidate-integrity changes in the shared service/claim path.

Coordination governance’s multi-instance and xtask production harnesses both pass; it is performing final candidate/goal evidence checks. Quality continues its scoped shared-core edits without touching main.

Quality’s current compile failure is explicit and local: the new `claim_with_quality_recovery` helper was placed in the trait implementation even though it is not a trait member. The agent is moving it to the inherent engine implementation; this is not being hidden as a provider failure. Coordination governance’s full-workflow rerun remains green.

Quality compiles cleanly after moving the helper to the correct engine implementation and is adding the two regression fixtures. Coordination governance is now checking Node identity/ownership semantics against the multi-instance harness.

Coordination governance found one remaining distributed-ownership defect: Node B can select or reconcile Node A’s claim because scheduling/recovery lacks an active-Node filter; the current two-daemon test uses the same default Node and misses it. I’m preserving `ec0d336`, adding a narrowly scoped distinct-Node round, and requeueing it through Refine.

Quality’s first multi-filter test invocation was syntactically invalid (exit 2); the same retry/context regressions pass when run as valid individual filters. Coordination round 8 is auditing all scheduler/reconciler Node checks against the preserved candidate before editing.

Quality’s focused behavior is correct, but the first full library run exposed six older fixtures that lacked the newly required candidate/failed-round evidence; the agent is updating those fixtures and race tests. Coordination round 8 remains active and unreviewed.

Quality’s remaining cancellation regression is failing because its helper process cannot see fixture Goal `GOAL-CANCEL`, so the managed Quality process never starts; the agent is correcting the test’s shared-state setup. Coordination compiles across all targets with the Node filters applied.

Quality has corrected the fixture visibility and restarted the full 435-test library gate. Coordination’s Node ownership changes remain compile-clean and are being paired with a distinct-Node daemon test.

Quality’s full library gate is green at 435/435 and it is running the ignored end-to-end daemon workflow. Coordination has added a focused mutation-boundary test proving foreign-Node claims are preserved.

Both new gates found actionable implementation/test issues rather than provider failures: coordination’s foreign-Node mutation test failed, and Quality’s daemon workflow failed under the new integrity gate. Both agents are inspecting preserved evidence and correcting the shared paths; nothing has been merged.
Quality corrected the daemon fixture and is at format/clippy. Coordination identified a test configuration mismatch around per-Node capacity and has adjusted the distinct-Node fixture.

Coordination’s focused foreign-Node boundary test now passes; it is adding a distinct-Node ignored case to the production multi-instance harness. Quality is rerunning the full library gate after clippy passed.

Quality’s implementation gates and its internal pre-commit audit are green; it is staging the scoped round-3 files for commit, after which Refine will still run the official independent governance step. Coordination’s first harness compile command stopped on formatting; it ran formatter and restarted the compile check.

Both active remediations are still within the existing workflow: Quality has its requested gates green and is finalizing its candidate, while coordination is finishing the distinct-node production-path coverage. I’m waiting for Refine’s official governance results before allowing either merge.

Quality is now under Refine’s independent governance review. That reviewer is checking an important recovery edge: detecting candidate mutation is insufficient if the failed Quality run leaves the preserved worktree dirty. Coordination’s new distinct-node test initially exposed a fixture issue, was corrected, and now passes; the implementation is proceeding through its remaining gates.

No authority decision is needed at this point. Coordination has entered its full serial regression suite after its focused ownership and recovery cases passed. Quality governance is still auditing cross-surface and concurrency behavior; I’m preserving that review boundary rather than treating the implementation agent’s own green report as approval.

Quality’s official review failed, and I’m surfacing the full boundary: four issues remain—mutation recovery, same-candidate concurrency control, production evidence for both paths, and missing Goal/Round/candidate context in the delegated agent prompt. I will keep the candidate isolated, append a remediation round, and retry through Refine’s shared workflow; I will not approve or merge it in this state.

The failed Quality candidate has been requeued as Round 4 with the four governance findings preserved verbatim in the remediation contract. Refine has claimed it normally. Coordination’s complete gate set is green—435 serial library tests plus full-workflow and both multi-instance suites—and its scoped commit is undergoing review; no merge has been attempted.

Round 4 is now implementing the remediation through the existing authorities: atomic Quality-operation ownership, workflow-claim/capacity coordination for standalone checks, and supervised Git restoration tied to the Quality operation. This keeps recovery and concurrency auditable instead of adding a parallel mechanism. Coordination remains isolated in review with no main-branch mutation.

Coordination’s pre-governance review found two concrete issues before official submission: `claim()` could return a foreign Node’s existing claim, and active-Node authority was checked non-atomically relative to mutation and lease release. Its transfer test also failed to exercise the intended state. The candidate remains unapproved; the workflow agent now has direct evidence to repair these paths within the same isolated round.

Quality’s first compile exposed an ordinary code issue, which the agent corrected; the shared Quality unit suite now passes. Coordination is extending the existing coordination lock across active-Node activation and workflow mutation rather than relying on a pre-check, and it is correcting the Goal-transfer test so the asserted transfer actually occurs. No provider or authentication failure has appeared.

Coordination’s corrected focused tests both pass, including the real two-daemon/distinct-Node path, and it is rerunning the full suite against the final lock semantics. Quality’s new production tests hit compile-only missing trait/type imports—not a runtime or provider failure—and the implementation path remains intact while those fixtures are corrected.

Quality now has the requested focused evidence green: mutation → supervised restoration → successful retry, failed restoration, full delegated prompt context, concurrent API/workflow ownership, and retry-queue integrity. Coordination’s entire executable matrix is green after the atomicity fix, and its amended commit is in a second read-only review before Refine’s own official governance stage.

Coordination’s second review still failed, so it remains blocked from approval. The remaining findings are: pause rollback is outside the coordination lease; the ownerless-claim test manufactures state through service calls instead of daemon scheduling; and the daemon test proves cancellation but not a Review transition or an explicit reviewed transfer/recovery boundary. These are evidence and atomicity failures, not hidden; the candidate stays isolated for another correction.

The latest focused coordination tests pass after putting pause rollback under the shared lease and making owner loss originate from the real daemon/provider path. Quality’s full library run reached 438/439; the only failure was the repository-wide prompt-size invariant. The agent compacted labels without dropping required context, restored the exact 700-word limit, and is rerunning all 439 tests.

Quality’s serial suite is now 439/439 and its ignored daemon workflow is 1/1. Clippy reported six style-only nested-condition warnings; the equivalent collapse is applied, and clippy plus the focused Quality suite now pass. Coordination has a final candidate `f239f28b` with its complete matrix green and is in one last read-only review; neither candidate has crossed the approval boundary.

Both implementation agents are in independent read-only review. One Quality review command was rejected because this installed CLI does not allow custom instructions with `review --uncommitted`; that command failure is visible and the agent switched to a read-only general review invocation. There is still no provider or authentication failure, and official Refine governance remains ahead of both candidates.

Both read-only reviews failed, so neither candidate will advance.

- Coordination: context logs/reports can still publish across an active-Node change, and foreign recovery silently returns `0` instead of an actionable ownership-transfer-required error.
- Quality: the proposed hard reset/clean was not proven to target only the workflow-owned isolated worktree; retry restoration and claim ownership were not atomic; promotion bypassed the shared Quality ownership check; and tests staged ownership instead of racing acquisition.

The destructive-scope finding was caught before merge. I am preserving the boundary: no primary worktree reset/clean is authorized, and the remediation must prove the exact isolated worktree identity before any recovery.

Coordination now distinguishes safe background observation from an explicit recovery attempt: unrelated foreign work is ignored, but attempting recovery of a transferred Goal with a foreign active claim returns an ownership-transfer conflict. All context evidence writes are also claim-authorized under the shared lease. Focused tests pass and the final full gate is running. Quality is implementing its isolation and atomic-handoff corrections.

Quality now rejects primary-checkout recovery outright and holds restoration, evidence, Goal state change, preserved timing, and claim creation under one shared transition boundary; automatic promotion uses the same boundary. Its first focused command was malformed because Cargo accepts one test filter, so the two tests were immediately split and run separately. That command error is visible and does not weaken the safety boundary.

Coordination has now exited implementation with candidate `ca98963a…` and entered Refine’s official `WorkflowImplementationGovernance` process. This is the authoritative review boundary; I’ll use its verdict, not the implementation agent’s self-review, before deciding whether normal approval is permitted. Quality’s focused promotion, mutation, restoration-failure, and retry tests currently pass.

Quality’s corrected focused suite is green: 20 Quality tests, five operation-registry tests, primary-worktree refusal, mutation recovery/retry, failed restoration, promotion exclusion, and a simultaneous API/workflow acquisition race. It has entered the full serial library gate. Official coordination governance is still inspecting surface-to-core Node activation paths and shared-root consistency.

Refine’s official coordination governance failed, and I’m preserving that authoritative result. The remaining defect is a live active-Node switch: an A-owned execution can be rejected after switching to B while recovery still sees its live runner and retained lease, stranding Goal/claim/capacity. The current tests never switch Nodes during a live provider run. I’m appending a narrow remediation round with that exact failure path and requeueing the isolated candidate; no approval or merge will occur.

Quality’s second independent audit still failed on two architecture points: standalone API checks could resolve stale/default capacity limits instead of the workflow’s authoritative policy, and reset/clean restoration was recorded but not itself cancellable supervised work. The agent accepted both findings and is centralizing policy resolution plus routing restoration through the process supervisor with operation-correlated evidence. The candidate remains uncommitted and unapproved.

Coordination is narrowing authority correctly: mutable active-Node selection still governs UI/API decisions, while an exact running commitment—claim, execution, decision version, Node, and owning runtime—retains only its publication/settlement authority. Recovery is being limited to ownerless commitments from the same runtime after a switch, without granting authority over another runtime’s claims. Quality is implementing supervised, cancellable restoration and authoritative workflow-policy lookup.

The new coordination failure-path test did its job: exact claim authority was retained inside workflow context, but the shared work-item service still rejected publication because its Goal-level check reread Node B. The agent is now composing exact execution authority through that existing service boundary rather than bypassing it. This is an ordinary focused-test failure, not a provider failure or a stranded live run.

Quality now has 22 focused tests green, including current shared-policy resolution, supervised recovery evidence, and cancellation terminating the correlated Git process; clippy also passes. Coordination is extending exact execution authority through the shared work-item service itself so reports and state transitions remain centralized instead of gaining a surface-specific bypass.

Coordination’s new real-daemon failure path passes: two A-owned providers start, the runtime switches to B, one finishes and one is terminated through the supervised registry, and both settle without a Running claim or capacity lease. Quality’s serial library, full workflow, and web suites are green again; its final independent audit is still running.

Quality’s final audit found one last real race: cancellation could occur between consecutive recovery commands and miss admission of the next supervised process. The fix is an operation-scoped admission lock so cancellation either blocks launch or waits for durable registration and then terminates the process. Coordination’s production and focused tests remain green; two compound verification commands returned nonzero only because of formatting and Cargo’s single-filter syntax, after which the separate tests passed.

Coordination’s broad matrix is green: 436 serial library tests, full workflow, and all three multi-instance cases, including live Node switch/provider loss. Quality’s cancellation-race test reached the intended conflict—second process admission was blocked—but the test incorrectly unwrapped that expected error; the agent is correcting the assertion and checking that cancellation is also persisted in Git audit evidence.

Coordination’s independent review caught an important distinction: merely changing the runtime’s selected Node must not strand the exact running commitment, but explicitly transferring the Goal’s ownership must still block the old worker. The shared automated-status service did not re-check that Goal ownership, so it is being tightened and the regression now covers both cases separately.

Both remediations are at their final evidence boundary again. Coordination has separate regressions for selection change versus explicit Goal transfer and is rerunning the complete multi-instance harness. Quality’s serial library and full workflow are green after the cancellation-admission lock, and a final read-only audit is checking that sole prior finding plus the preserved safety invariants.
Daemon restarted before the provider turn completed.
Sent 18 queued messages to the provider.

I’m taking over at the two explicit lost-worker stalls. I’ll inspect their durable goal/round evidence and the current Refine process state first, then use only Refine’s normal retry/recovery path where the evidence supports it; I won’t merge, delete, or rewrite worktrees.

The durable snapshot has already changed from the supplied evidence: Refine’s workflow runner is live again, but there is no separate goal worker visible yet. I’m checking the two goals’ latest rounds and activity logs before deciding whether an explicit retry is still needed, because replaying while a recovered worker owns the goal would violate the coordination boundary.

Refine’s own recovery has now settled both orphaned executions as interrupted failures, released all active claims, and reports the automation queue idle. The evidence explicitly says “restart the Goal when ready,” so I’m checking the supported restart command and the preserved candidate/worktree cleanliness; I will not touch the separate older failed goal or the human-review item.

The supported recovery is Refine’s “Last workflow state” action, which returns interrupted implementation failures to `todo`. Refine has a regression test for reusing the existing round worktree on retry, so the uncommitted agent changes are preserved. I’m applying that exact selective action to only the two lost-worker goals, then I’ll monitor their provider, governance, and terminal state.
Daemon restarted before the provider turn completed.
Sent 281 queued messages to the provider.

I’m reconciling the latest durable goal state with the live process/runner state first. I’ll only retry work through Refine’s existing supervised workflow path, and I’ll preserve the three recorded failures and any worktrees until their evidence is understood.

I’ve separated the duplicated polling messages from the authoritative state. The durable picture is still two queued goals, no live workflow agent, and one separate failed goal; I’m now checking the pause/retry controls and each goal’s preserved evidence before restarting anything.

Refine’s own CLI exposes the exact bounded controls we need: workflow pause/resume and goal retry. I’m using those interfaces, not editing durable JSON. The next check is whether “resume” is enough to reclaim the two queued goals or whether their prior worker-loss evidence requires explicit per-goal retry.

The durable store is newer than the repeated chat projection: both queued goals are presently marked `in-progress` with active claims, but there is still no daemon registered under `refine-next/run`. That mismatch is the core issue. I’m checking the actual self-hosted runtime and managed-process records before deciding whether these are live claims or ghost claims; I will not “resume” over an existing owner.

The apparent stall has recovered through Refine’s existing runner: both goals now have fresh running claims and live Codex child processes, started at 19:07 UTC. I did not issue a duplicate resume or retry. I’ll watch their durable transitions and surface any provider/governance failure verbatim.

Both claims remain healthy and live; no state transition or provider error has occurred. The previously failed autonomous-recovery goal remains intentionally untouched because its latest round explicitly parks behind the shared-coordination goal and failed governance on evidence integrity.

The shared-coordination worker is actively running its focused Rust test suite; the Quality worker is still live with a substantial preserved diff. This is real progress rather than a ghost claim, so intervention would risk duplicate ownership.

interruption_detail: Daemon restarted before the provider turn completed.
